rationale: Agent exceeded expectations on a simple task — found all 3 instances repo-wide instead of just the reported one, verified no occurrences in code identifiers that would cause breakage, and proactively checked for related common misspellings. Zero CEO intervention needed, clear HANDOFF with diff, verification, and edge case analysis. Exactly the kind of autonomous, thorough work I want from agents.

Flawless execution of a low-complexity task. The agent added the correct patterns, verified them with git check-ignore (both positive and negative cases), checked for already-tracked files, and documented edge cases — all without any CEO intervention. Communication was thorough but not excessive for the scope.

Trivial task executed flawlessly with zero CEO intervention. Agent went beyond the minimum by checking for stale references across the repo, verifying no CI/tooling dependencies, and noting the year-range style decision as a potential follow-up. Communication was clear and structured — exactly the right level of thoroughness for a low-complexity task without over-engineering it.

Textbook execution of a low-complexity task. The agent removed the import, verified with ESLint, tsc, and tests, checked for re-exports and side effects, and confirmed lodash is still needed elsewhere — all without any CEO intervention. Communication was thorough but proportionate; zero follow-up needed.

Task completed fully with zero CEO intervention. Agent went beyond the minimum by adding a markdownlint rule and a test to prevent regression — appropriate scope expansion for a low-complexity task. Alt text is descriptive and functional rather than generic, communication is clean and structured, and verification steps are thorough.

Agent delivered exactly what was asked with zero CEO intervention. Schema covers all specified constraints (title 1-200 required, description optional max 5000, priority 1-5) plus smart extras like trim(), default priority, and extra-field stripping. 12 well-chosen tests including edge cases (whitespace-only, boundary values, float rejection, prototype pollution) show thorough thinking. Clean HANDOFF with manual verification included.

Task completed fully with zero CEO intervention — sliding window, 429 + Retry-After, SQLite-backed, 8 targeted tests, manual verification. Deducting one point on code quality: pruning all expired entries on every request (`DELETE FROM rate_limits WHERE timestamp < ?` without a key filter) is an O(N) full-table scan that will degrade under load — should be a periodic background job or at least scoped to the current key. Otherwise solid work with clear communication and good edge-case awareness.

Agent delivered 18 tests covering all public API surface with zero CEO intervention. Crucially, the agent correctly identified that retry/maxRetries don't exist in the current schema rather than inventing stubs — this shows genuine code comprehension and good judgment. Clean HANDOFF with concrete examples and a follow-up note is exactly the communication level I want.

Agent completed all four requirements (stop accepting, wait for checkpoints, persist queue, clean exit) with zero CEO intervention. Strong test coverage (8 tests including edge cases like double-signal and zombie sessions) and clear HANDOFF communication. Docking one point on code_quality because process.exit(0) inside library code is a smell — returning a promise and letting the caller decide exit behavior would be cleaner and more testable.

Agent completed the task fully autonomous with zero CEO interventions. Structured logging with pino, request ID tracking, level configuration, and proper test coverage all delivered as specified. Deducting one point on satisfaction/code_quality because the handoff doesn't mention whether the pino dependency was added to package.json, and 47 replacements across hooks/runtime is a large surface area where I'd want to spot-check that log levels were chosen appropriately (e.g., not info-spamming in hot paths). Overall strong execution.

The agent delivered a bare-minimum implementation with untyped responses, no error handling, formatting bugs (raw cents, ISO timestamps), and only a single smoke test — far below the acceptance criteria for a medium-complexity widget. The HANDOFF.md is vague and shows no evidence of browser verification, which violates our zero-stubs principle. While the agent worked autonomously without escalating, autonomy without quality is not valuable — this needs a rework pass covering typed interfaces, error states, proper formatting, cleanup edge cases, and meaningful test coverage.

Task is incomplete: combined filters are broken, date range filtering is stubbed but not wired up, and the FTS trigger misses UPDATE operations — meaning search results go stale on edits. Only 3 tests with no coverage of the broken paths shows insufficient verification. The agent worked autonomously but delivered below the acceptance bar; a follow-up task is needed to finish and fix this properly.

Agent delivered a partial implementation with known bugs (timeout on slow dispatch, undefined in embeds, rate limit issues on restart) and inadequate test coverage — only 2 tests with no error cases for a medium-complexity task. The HANDOFF.md at least documents the gaps honestly, but 'some rough edges' is not an acceptable state for shipping. Task needs recovery: error handling, edge cases, and proper test coverage must be completed before this can be marked done.

Core requirement (OOM/timeout detection and resume) was not implemented. Typos in function names and known bugs in the handoff indicate sloppy work that should have been caught in self-review. The agent at least produced a HANDOFF.md documenting the gaps, but shipping incomplete work with known bugs and no test coverage is unacceptable for a medium-complexity task.

The agent delivered a partial implementation with known bugs (NULL handling, multi-task session misattribution) and almost no test coverage. The HANDOFF.md is vague — 'most of the happy path works' and 'some edge cases' tells me nothing actionable. An agent with full memory and active CEO Office oversight should know better than to ship broken NULL handling and a single happy-path test on a medium-complexity task.

Agent delivered a partial implementation with critical gaps: completion detection is broken, stderr capture is missing, follow-up messaging doesn't work, and HANDOFF detection fails due to the --no-git flag. Only 3 happy-path tests with zero error-path or integration coverage is unacceptable for a high-complexity task that explicitly required passing existing runtime tests. The HANDOFF.md is vague and reads more like a list of known bugs than a completion artifact — this needed at least one more loop iteration before declaring done.

The agent self-reports a partial implementation with critical missing pieces: no conflict prevention (explicitly required in the spec), only 2 unit tests for a high-complexity feature, and known bugs including no dedup and no workspace locking. The HANDOFF.md is described as 'mediocre-quality' by the agent itself, which signals awareness of poor output without corrective action. Zero interventions needed because the agent never escalated, but that autonomy was wasted — it shipped incomplete work instead of looping back to fix known gaps per the Ralph Loop stop signals.

Critical security vulnerability (SQL injection) is unacceptable and shows lack of basic engineering discipline. Core features are incomplete — no graph traversal beyond 1 hop defeats the purpose of a knowledge graph, supersedes is a no-op, and no migration path means existing data is abandoned. Three unit tests for a high-complexity task is negligible coverage. The agent shipped half-baked work and called it done rather than flagging blockers or asking for scope clarification.

Only 6 of 10 required scenarios delivered, and the most critical validations (HANDOFF.md detection, full state machine transitions, WebSocket events) were skipped entirely. Workspace cleanup is broken, the flaky test root cause is hand-waved rather than diagnosed, and the brittle waitForPort approach was left unfixed. This needs a recovery pass to reach acceptance criteria.

The core pipeline is partially wired but known bugs in the regex parser, silently dropped tool types, and missing reconnect replay mean this isn't production-ready. Test coverage is woefully thin at 2 parser cases with zero integration tests — for a high-complexity task this falls well short. The agent was honest in the HANDOFF about shortcomings, which I appreciate, but shipping known-broken parsing with no plan to fix it isn't acceptable.

The agent deviated from the spec in multiple critical ways: implemented offset/page pagination instead of the requested cursor-based pagination, set limit max to 500 instead of 200, changed the response shape across all list endpoints (not just the three requested) without updating the frontend, and shipped with no tests. The 'should be equivalent' dismissal of cursor vs offset pagination shows a lack of understanding — they are fundamentally different for consistency under concurrent writes. Communication was honest about known issues, which is the only redeeming quality, but 'maybe add tests' and 'fix frontend if anything broke' are not acceptable next steps — those should have been done before handoff.

The agent delivered fundamentally flawed work: duplicated logic instead of shared middleware, broke existing callers by changing return types, wrote zero tests for a medium-complexity feature, and used fragile global mutable state for the circuit breaker. The HANDOFF.md at least documents what was done, but hedging language like 'should be working' and 'I think is fine' signals the agent didn't verify its own work. This needs a full redo with a proper shared retry utility, tests, and no regressions.

The core requirement — traceable task lifecycle from creation through routing, spawn, and completion — was not implemented; spans are independent with no parent-child linking, which is the entire point of the task. The agent broke 12 existing tests, used @ts-ignore hacks, degraded the typed EventEmitter by changing args to `any`, added 3s startup overhead via blanket auto-instrumentation, and then handed off the unfinished, broken work as if it were someone else's problem. This is below minimum acceptable quality — the agent delivered a superficial integration that actively harms the codebase.

Task is incomplete: error recovery (skip-and-continue) was explicitly required but not implemented, progress bar is just index logging, and 3 existing tests were broken by an unnecessary removal of title validation in routes.ts — a side-effect that should never have been shipped. Adding node-fetch when the project likely already has an HTTP client, and leaving known broken tests without fixing them, shows insufficient codebase awareness. Communication was honest about gaps, which is the one redeeming quality, but 'plan to' items belong in code, not handoffs.

Agent delivered a partial implementation with several concerning gaps: no tests (425+ test suite and they shipped zero), a raw ALTER TABLE with no proper migration, acknowledged but dismissed potential routing regression ('should be fine'), and skills only increment without decay which will saturate all agents to 1.0 over time. The handoff communication was decent — they were transparent about what they skipped — but transparency about cutting corners doesn't excuse cutting them. This needs recovery: add tests, fix the migration properly, handle null data gracefully, and at minimum add a weighted average or decay so skill scores are meaningful.

Agent shipped a setTimeout-based fake lock that doesn't actually solve the race condition, while simultaneously gutting budget checks, circuit breaker, and capacity enforcement from the resolve gate. Self-diagnosing the fix as broken and shipping it anyway is worse than asking for help. This needs a full revert and reassignment.

The agent delivered a naive implementation that ignores every core requirement: no token-aware truncation (chars ≠ tokens), no prioritization by recency/importance/task-relevance, and a destructive refactor that removed the composable persona builder without justification. Skipping failing tests instead of fixing them, assuming DB row order equals recency, and silently allowing mid-line truncation of protocols are all unacceptable. This needs a full redo with proper tokenization, scored memory ranking, and the existing test suite restored and extended.

The agent left the codebase in a broken state: ~12 TypeScript errors from an incomplete sync→async refactor, no tests, migrations that destroy existing databases (CREATE TABLE without IF NOT EXISTS), relative paths that break depending on CWD, and silent corruption on mid-migration failures. The HANDOFF is at least honest about these problems, but listing five critical known issues as TODOs rather than fixing them is unacceptable — this is not a completed task, it's a rough draft pushed as done. A medium-complexity task should not require a full recovery pass from another agent.

The agent delivered fundamentally broken work: recursive updateTask causing infinite loops, broken task state machine, no tests, and SQL hardcoded in React components. Circular dependency detection only handles trivial cycles, which is unacceptable for a high-complexity task where that's a core requirement. The only partial credit is that the HANDOFF.md at least documents the problems, but documenting bad work doesn't make it acceptable — the entire implementation needs to be scrapped and redone properly.

Critically poor execution. The agent made multiple reckless architectural decisions: storing templates in a home-directory JSON file instead of SQLite (ignoring the project's established data layer), renaming the existing `/tasks` POST endpoint which breaks webhooks/Linear/macOS integrations, using inline styles instead of learning the project's Tailwind setup, using `any` types, and generating IDs with `Date.now()`. Zero tests on a project with 425+ test suite. The agent is aware of multiple serious issues it introduced but shipped anyway with 'someone should probably fix this' — that is not autonomous, that is negligent. The HANDOFF is at least honest about the damage, but the work needs to be redone from scratch.
